{{ill|Shikinai Ronsha|lt=Shikinai Ronsha|ja|論社|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '栃木県栃木市岩舟町小野寺4697', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Murahi Shrine}} [[JA:村檜神社]] {{wikidata link|Q30935930}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Murahi Shrine |Image = [[File:Murahi shrine.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Tochigi Prefecture|Q44843|WD=Q44843|1=Tochigi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Tochigi City|Q44830|WD=Q44830|1=Tochigi (city)}} Iwafune-chō Onodera 4697 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} |Founded = Traditionally {{ill|lt=Taika|Q1077930|WD=Q1077930|1=Taika (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=646|Q33285|WD=Q33285|1=646}}) |Main hall = Three-bay {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}} |Alternate names = {{plainlist| * Onodera Hachiman{{Sfn|Tochigi Prefectural Education History Compilation Committee|1957|p=428}} * Murahi Hachiman{{Sfn|Tochigi City History Compilation Committee|1979|p=738}} }} |Pilgrimage = |Annual festival = |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Tochigi|label=Murahi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Murahi Shrine''' ('''Murahi Jinja'''; むらひじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|lt=Tochigi Prefecture|Q44843|WD=Q44843|1=Tochigi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Tochigi City|Q44830|WD=Q44830|1=Tochigi (city)}}. Under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a village shrine (gōsha). It claims the title of the Third Shrine of Shimotsuke Province. === History === According to shrine tradition, on {{ill|lt=Taika|Q1077930|WD=Q1077930|1=Taika (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=646|Q33285|WD=Q33285|1=646}}) {{ill|lt=September 29 (lunar calendar)|Q840263|WD=Q840263|lt=September 29|1=September 29 (Lunisolar)}}{{Sfn|Kazayama|1903|p=59}}, {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|lt=the Kumano deity|1=Kumano Gongen}} and {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054|lt=the Hie deity|1=Sannō Gongen}} ({{ill|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui no Kami|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}) were invited and enshrined{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}{{Sfn|Ishihara|1964|p=198}}. Ōyamakui no Kami is the deity of {{ill|lt=Mount Hiei|Q748040|WD=Q748040|1=Mount Hiei}}, and its enshrinement here has been noted in connection with the nearby {{ill|lt=Daijiji (Tochigi City)|Q11435473|WD=Q11435473|lt=Onodera-san Daijiji Temple|1=Daiji-ji Temple}}{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. The surrounding areas—{{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Onodera|UNKNOWN|lt=Onodera}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Kamioka|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamioka}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Shimo-oka|UNKNOWN|lt=Shimo-oka}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Mitani|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitani}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Niisato|UNKNOWN|lt=Niisato}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Furue|UNKNOWN|lt=Furue}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Shimotsuhara|Q109369623|WD=Q109369623|lt=Shimotsuhara|1=Shimotsuhara}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Tatamioka|UNKNOWN|lt=Tatamioka}}, Komaba (now part of {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Shizu|Q11662858|WD=Q11662858|lt=Shizu|1=Iwafunemachi Shizuka}}), and {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Washinosu|Q11676262|WD=Q11676262|lt=Washinosu|1=Iwafunemachi Washinosu}}—were collectively called the “Ten Villages of Onodera,” and the shrine is said to have been founded as their guardian shrine{{Sfn|Ishihara|1964|p=198}}. In {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daido|1=Daidō}} 2 ({{ill|lt=807|Q30016|WD=Q30016|1=807}}), the enshrined deity {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, originally worshiped at the Hachiman Shrine of Onoguchi in {{ill|lt=Minagawa Village|Q11580946|WD=Q11580946|1=Minagawa}}, which had been merged, was welcomed as the principal deity. Furthermore, during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243|1=Emperor Seiwa}} ({{ill|lt=858|Q30371|WD=Q30371|1=858}}–{{ill|lt=876|Q23815|WD=Q23815|1=876}}), the Hachiman deity that had been invited to Hachimanzawa in Minagawa Village was, during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōkō|Q440518|WD=Q440518|1=Emperor Kōkō}} ({{ill|lt=884|Q23895|WD=Q23895|1=884}}–{{ill|lt=887|Q25010|WD=Q25010|1=887}}), merged into Murahi Shrine on the grounds that its original site was impure{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}{{Sfn|Ishihara|1964|p=198}}. In {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340|1=Tengyō}} 2 ({{ill|lt=939|Q23422|WD=Q23422|1=939}}), {{ill|lt=Fujiwara no Hidesato|Q1136623|WD=Q1136623|1=Fujiwara no Hidesato}}, who went on to subdue {{ill|lt=Taira no Masakado|Q1207652|WD=Q1207652|1=Taira no Masakado}}, is said to have prayed here for victory{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. There is also a theory that the shrine itself was founded by Hidesato. In Kōno Morihiro’s *Shimotsuke Kokushi*, it is stated that Hidesato, as lord of {{ill|lt=Karasawayama Castle|Q11418602|WD=Q11418602|1=Karasawa Castle}}, founded Murahi Shrine about one *ri* away to the northeast (the demon gate) to protect the castle{{Sfn|Kōno|1893|p=12}}{{Sfn|Suzuka|1902|p=294}}. Some sources describe this as a “reconstruction”{{Sfn|Tochigi Prefectural Education History Compilation Committee|1957|p=428}}. From the medieval period onward, the shrine received devotion from the {{ill|lt=Shimotsuke Onodera clan|Q16141519|WD=Q16141519|1=Shimotsuke Onodera clan}} and the {{ill|lt=Sano clan|Q11385058|WD=Q11385058|1=Sano clan}}, lords of Karasawayama Castle{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. During the Edo period, it was also revered by merchants of Tochigi Town, where {{ill|lt=Tochigi-juku|Q30926264|WD=Q30926264|1=Tochigi-juku}} was established{{Sfn|Higano|1966|p=50}}. In front of the shrine buildings stands a pair of Oribe lanterns dedicated in {{ill|lt=Kyōhō|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 11, 12th month ({{ill|lt=1726|Q6936|WD=Q6936|1=1726}} December or January of the following year) by the Noguchi family, hemp wholesalers of “Tsukegi Kamimachi” (present-day {{ill|lt=Mannmachi (Tochigi City)|Q11353890|WD=Q11353890|lt=Mannmachi|1=Yorozuchō}} in Tochigi City){{Sfn|Tochigi City History Compilation Committee|1978|pp=527–528}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}), the shrine was designated a village shrine (gōsha) under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Records Compilation Office|1912|p=46}}. === Cultural Properties === === Important Cultural Property (National Designation) === * '''Main Hall''' : Built in {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|1=Tenbun}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1533|Q6443|WD=Q6443|1=1533}}){{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. According to some records, part of it was renovated in {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} 21 ({{ill|lt=1414|Q6179|WD=Q6179|1=1414}}), and a major renovation was carried out in Tenbun 2 by {{ill|lt=Sano Toyotsuna|Q11385105|WD=Q11385105|1=Sano Toyotsuna}}, lord of {{ill|lt=Sano Castle|Q11384983|WD=Q11384983|1=Sano Castle}}{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. A carving of a {{ill|lt=melon|Q5881191|WD=Q5881191|1=Melon}}, said to have been embedded by the master carpenter {{ill|lt=Hidari Jingorō|Q2668220|WD=Q2668220|1=Hidari Jingorō}} at the time of construction or major renovation, remains on the upper part of a side pillar on the southwest side of the hall{{Sfn|Kazayama|1903|p=59}}{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. : It is a three-bay Kasuga-zukuri structure with a cypress-bark roof{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. There is no distinction between the worship hall and the main hall{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. The {{ill|lt=bracket complexes|Q7814068|WD=Q7814068|lt=to-gumi|1=Tokyō (architecture)}} above the pillars supporting the front eaves are noted for unique features not seen in other shrine buildings of the mid-to-late Muromachi period, which was key to its cultural property designation{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. : On {{ill|lt=August 1|Q2788|WD=Q2788|1=August 1}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 41), it was designated a Specially Protected Building under the then {{ill|lt=Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law|Q11411552|WD=Q11411552|1=Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law}} (equivalent to an {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} under the {{ill|lt=Law for the Protection of Cultural Properties|Q1188502|WD=Q1188502|1=Cultural Properties Protection Law}})Home Ministry Notification No. 76 of August 1, Meiji 41 (see: [{{NDLDC|2950877}} National Diet Library Digital Collection]). === Tochigi City Designated Natural Monument === * '''Shrine Grove''' : Designated as a natural monument of {{ill|lt=Iwafune Town|Q1203561|WD=Q1203561|1=Iwafune, Tochigi}} on {{ill|lt=April 1|Q2510|WD=Q2510|1=April 1}}, {{ill|lt=1972|Q2476|WD=Q2476|1=1972}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 47){{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}. Following the municipal merger on {{ill|lt=April 5|Q2507|WD=Q2507|1=April 5}}, {{ill|lt=2014|Q1999|WD=Q1999|1=2014}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 26), it was inherited by Tochigi City{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}. === Grounds === File:Murahi-jinja Main Hall.JPG|Main hall File:Murahi-jinja Main Hall tablet.JPG|Plaque of the worship hall File:Murahi-jinja Torii.JPG|Torii gate File:Murahi-jinja Kagura Hall.JPG|Kagura hall File:Murahi-jinja Shrine office.JPG|Shrine gate File:Murahi-jinja jukuyo-tou.JPG|Jūkyoya-tō stone monument === Subsidiary Shrines === Of the 11 subsidiary shrines, 8 are enshrined together in the subordinate shrine building of Nishinomiya Shrine. Orihime Shrine was destroyed by fire, and Itsukushima Shrine collapsed and has not been rebuilt. * Nishinomiya Shrine * Sumiyoshi Shrine * Raiden Shrine / Atago Shrine File:Murahi-jinja Nishinomiya-jinja.JPG|Nishinomiya Shrine torii File:Murahi-jinja Nishinomiya-jinja.JPG Main Hall.JPG|Nishinomiya Shrine hall File:村檜神社 西宮神社 末社.jpg|Nishinomiya Shrine subordinate shrines File:Murahi-jinja Atago-jinja.JPG|Raiden Shrine / Atago Shrine File:Murahi-jinja Shumiyoshi-jinja.JPG|Sumiyoshi Shrine === Access === * 15 minutes by car from {{ill|lt=Iwafune Station|Q11475586|WD=Q11475586|1=Iwafune Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Ryōmō Line|Q253391|WD=Q253391|lt=JR Ryōmō Line|1=Ryōmō Line}} * About 25 minutes by car from {{ill|lt=Sano-Fujioka Interchange|Q10885981|WD=Q10885981|lt=Sano-Fujioka I.C.|1=Sano-Fujioka interchange}} on the {{ill|lt=Tōhoku Expressway|Q428428|WD=Q428428|1=Tōhoku Expressway}} * 40 minutes from Tochigi Station on the JR Ryōmō Line / {{ill|lt=Tōbu Nikkō Line|Q845954|WD=Q845954|1=Tōbu Nikkō Line}} via the Tochigi City Fureai Bus Minagawa Hinokuchi Line bound for “Yurarakukan” ** Get off at “Murahi Shrine Entrance” bus stop, 5-minute walk (few services) ** Get off at “Eda Clinic-mae” bus stop, 20-minute walk === Notes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book |Japanese |editor=Shigehide Ishihara Education |date=1974-03-25 |title=History of Iwafune Town |publisher=Iwafune Town |ref={{SfnRef|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974}} }} * {{Cite book |Japanese |editor=Hiroo Kazayama |date=1903-04-20 |title=History of Shrines in Shimotsuke: Volume 4 |publisher=Hiroo Kazayama |page=59 |ref={{SfnRef|Kazayama|1903}} }}{{National Diet Library Digital Collection|815435/1/61|format=NDLJP}} * {{Cite book |Japanese |editor=Morihiro Kōno |date=1893-10 |title=Shimotsuke Kokushi: Vol. 3 |publisher=Satō San’ichirō |page=12 |ref={{SfnRef|Kōno|1893}} Shrine}} [[JA:村檜神社]] {{wikidata link|Q30935930}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Murahi Shrine |Image = [[File:Murahi shrine.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Tochigi Prefecture|Q44843|WD=Q44843|1=Tochigi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Tochigi City|Q44830|WD=Q44830|1=Tochigi (city)}} Iwafune-chō Onodera 4697 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} |Founded = Traditionally {{ill|lt=Taika|Q1077930|WD=Q1077930|1=Taika (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=646|Q33285|WD=Q33285|1=646}}) |Main hall = Three-bay {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}} |Alternate names = {{plainlist| * Onodera Hachiman{{Sfn|Tochigi Prefectural Education History Compilation Committee|1957|p=428}} * Murahi Hachiman{{Sfn|Tochigi City History Compilation Committee|1979|p=738}} }} |Pilgrimage = |Annual festival = |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Tochigi|label=Murahi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Murahi Shrine''' ('''Murahi Jinja'''; むらひじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|lt=Tochigi Prefecture|Q44843|WD=Q44843|1=Tochigi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Tochigi City|Q44830|WD=Q44830|1=Tochigi (city)}}. Under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a village shrine (gōsha). It claims the title of the Third Shrine of Shimotsuke Province. === History === According to shrine tradition, on {{ill|lt=Taika|Q1077930|WD=Q1077930|1=Taika (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=646|Q33285|WD=Q33285|1=646}}) {{ill|lt=September 29 (lunar calendar)|Q840263|WD=Q840263|lt=September 29|1=September 29 (Lunisolar)}}{{Sfn|Kazayama|1903|p=59}}, {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|lt=the Kumano deity|1=Kumano Gongen}} and {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054|lt=the Hie deity|1=Sannō Gongen}} ({{ill|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui no Kami|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}) were invited and enshrined{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}{{Sfn|Ishihara|1964|p=198}}. Ōyamakui no Kami is the deity of {{ill|lt=Mount Hiei|Q748040|WD=Q748040|1=Mount Hiei}}, and its enshrinement here has been noted in connection with the nearby {{ill|lt=Daijiji (Tochigi City)|Q11435473|WD=Q11435473|lt=Onodera-san Daijiji Temple|1=Daiji-ji Temple}}{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. The surrounding areas—{{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Onodera|UNKNOWN|lt=Onodera}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Kamioka|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamioka}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Shimo-oka|UNKNOWN|lt=Shimo-oka}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Mitani|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitani}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Niisato|UNKNOWN|lt=Niisato}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Furue|UNKNOWN|lt=Furue}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Shimotsuhara|Q109369623|WD=Q109369623|lt=Shimotsuhara|1=Shimotsuhara}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Tatamioka|UNKNOWN|lt=Tatamioka}}, Komaba (now part of {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Shizu|Q11662858|WD=Q11662858|lt=Shizu|1=Iwafunemachi Shizuka}}), and {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Washinosu|Q11676262|WD=Q11676262|lt=Washinosu|1=Iwafunemachi Washinosu}}—were collectively called the “Ten Villages of Onodera,” and the shrine is said to have been founded as their guardian shrine{{Sfn|Ishihara|1964|p=198}}. In {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daido|1=Daidō}} 2 ({{ill|lt=807|Q30016|WD=Q30016|1=807}}), the enshrined deity {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, originally worshiped at the Hachiman Shrine of Onoguchi in {{ill|lt=Minagawa Village|Q11580946|WD=Q11580946|1=Minagawa}}, which had been merged, was welcomed as the principal deity. Furthermore, during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243|1=Emperor Seiwa}} ({{ill|lt=858|Q30371|WD=Q30371|1=858}}–{{ill|lt=876|Q23815|WD=Q23815|1=876}}), the Hachiman deity that had been invited to Hachimanzawa in Minagawa Village was, during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōkō|Q440518|WD=Q440518|1=Emperor Kōkō}} ({{ill|lt=884|Q23895|WD=Q23895|1=884}}–{{ill|lt=887|Q25010|WD=Q25010|1=887}}), merged into Murahi Shrine on the grounds that its original site was impure{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}{{Sfn|Ishihara|1964|p=198}}. In {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340|1=Tengyō}} 2 ({{ill|lt=939|Q23422|WD=Q23422|1=939}}), {{ill|lt=Fujiwara no Hidesato|Q1136623|WD=Q1136623|1=Fujiwara no Hidesato}}, who went on to subdue {{ill|lt=Taira no Masakado|Q1207652|WD=Q1207652|1=Taira no Masakado}}, is said to have prayed here for victory{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. There is also a theory that the shrine itself was founded by Hidesato. In Kōno Morihiro’s *Shimotsuke Kokushi*, it is stated that Hidesato, as lord of {{ill|lt=Karasawayama Castle|Q11418602|WD=Q11418602|1=Karasawa Castle}}, founded Murahi Shrine about one *ri* away to the northeast (the demon gate) to protect the castle{{Sfn|Kōno|1893|p=12}}{{Sfn|Suzuka|1902|p=294}}. Some sources describe this as a “reconstruction”{{Sfn|Tochigi Prefectural Education History Compilation Committee|1957|p=428}}. From the medieval period onward, the shrine received devotion from the {{ill|lt=Shimotsuke Onodera clan|Q16141519|WD=Q16141519|1=Shimotsuke Onodera clan}} and the {{ill|lt=Sano clan|Q11385058|WD=Q11385058|1=Sano clan}}, lords of Karasawayama Castle{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. During the Edo period, it was also revered by merchants of Tochigi Town, where {{ill|lt=Tochigi-juku|Q30926264|WD=Q30926264|1=Tochigi-juku}} was established{{Sfn|Higano|1966|p=50}}. In front of the shrine buildings stands a pair of Oribe lanterns dedicated in {{ill|lt=Kyōhō|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 11, 12th month ({{ill|lt=1726|Q6936|WD=Q6936|1=1726}} December or January of the following year) by the Noguchi family, hemp wholesalers of “Tsukegi Kamimachi” (present-day {{ill|lt=Mannmachi (Tochigi City)|Q11353890|WD=Q11353890|lt=Mannmachi|1=Yorozuchō}} in Tochigi City){{Sfn|Tochigi City History Compilation Committee|1978|pp=527–528}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}), the shrine was designated a village shrine (gōsha) under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Records Compilation Office|1912|p=46}}. === Cultural Properties === === Important Cultural Property (National Designation) === * '''Main Hall''' : Built in {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|1=Tenbun}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1533|Q6443|WD=Q6443|1=1533}}){{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. According to some records, part of it was renovated in {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} 21 ({{ill|lt=1414|Q6179|WD=Q6179|1=1414}}), and a major renovation was carried out in Tenbun 2 by {{ill|lt=Sano Toyotsuna|Q11385105|WD=Q11385105|1=Sano Toyotsuna}}, lord of {{ill|lt=Sano Castle|Q11384983|WD=Q11384983|1=Sano Castle}}{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. A carving of a {{ill|lt=melon|Q5881191|WD=Q5881191|1=Melon}}, said to have been embedded by the master carpenter {{ill|lt=Hidari Jingorō|Q2668220|WD=Q2668220|1=Hidari Jingorō}} at the time of construction or major renovation, remains on the upper part of a side pillar on the southwest side of the hall{{Sfn|Kazayama|1903|p=59}}{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. : It is a three-bay Kasuga-zukuri structure with a cypress-bark roof{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. There is no distinction between the worship hall and the main hall{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. The {{ill|lt=bracket complexes|Q7814068|WD=Q7814068|lt=to-gumi|1=Tokyō (architecture)}} above the pillars supporting the front eaves are noted for unique features not seen in other shrine buildings of the mid-to-late Muromachi period, which was key to its cultural property designation{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. : On {{ill|lt=August 1|Q2788|WD=Q2788|1=August 1}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 41), it was designated a Specially Protected Building under the then {{ill|lt=Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law|Q11411552|WD=Q11411552|1=Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law}} (equivalent to an {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} under the {{ill|lt=Law for the Protection of Cultural Properties|Q1188502|WD=Q1188502|1=Cultural Properties Protection Law}})Home Ministry Notification No. 76 of August 1, Meiji 41 (see: [{{NDLDC|2950877}} National Diet Library Digital Collection]). === Tochigi City Designated Natural Monument === * '''Shrine Grove''' : Designated as a natural monument of {{ill|lt=Iwafune Town|Q1203561|WD=Q1203561|1=Iwafune, Tochigi}} on {{ill|lt=April 1|Q2510|WD=Q2510|1=April 1}}, {{ill|lt=1972|Q2476|WD=Q2476|1=1972}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 47){{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}. Following the municipal merger on {{ill|lt=April 5|Q2507|WD=Q2507|1=April 5}}, {{ill|lt=2014|Q1999|WD=Q1999|1=2014}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 26), it was inherited by Tochigi City{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}. === Grounds === File:Murahi-jinja Main Hall.JPG|Main hall File:Murahi-jinja Main Hall tablet.JPG|Plaque of the worship hall File:Murahi-jinja Torii.JPG|Torii gate File:Murahi-jinja Kagura Hall.JPG|Kagura hall File:Murahi-jinja Shrine office.JPG|Shrine gate File:Murahi-jinja jukuyo-tou.JPG|Jūkyoya-tō stone monument === Subsidiary Shrines === Of the 11 subsidiary shrines, 8 are enshrined together in the subordinate shrine building of Nishinomiya Shrine. Orihime Shrine was destroyed by fire, and Itsukushima Shrine collapsed and has not been rebuilt. * Nishinomiya Shrine * Sumiyoshi Shrine * Raiden Shrine / Atago Shrine File:Murahi-jinja Nishinomiya-jinja.JPG|Nishinomiya Shrine torii File:Murahi-jinja Nishinomiya-jinja.JPG Main Hall.JPG|Nishinomiya Shrine hall File:村檜神社 西宮神社 末社.jpg|Nishinomiya Shrine subordinate shrines File:Murahi-jinja Atago-jinja.JPG|Raiden Shrine / Atago Shrine File:Murahi-jinja Shumiyoshi-jinja.JPG|Sumiyoshi Shrine === Access === * 15 minutes by car from {{ill|lt=Iwafune Station|Q11475586|WD=Q11475586|1=Iwafune Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Ryōmō Line|Q253391|WD=Q253391|lt=JR Ryōmō Line|1=Ryōmō Line}} * About 25 minutes by car from {{ill|lt=Sano-Fujioka Interchange|Q10885981|WD=Q10885981|lt=Sano-Fujioka I.C.|1=Sano-Fujioka interchange}} on the {{ill|lt=Tōhoku Expressway|Q428428|WD=Q428428|1=Tōhoku Expressway}} * 40 minutes from Tochigi Station on the JR Ryōmō Line / {{ill|lt=Tōbu Nikkō Line|Q845954|WD=Q845954|1=Tōbu Nikkō Line}} via the Tochigi City Fureai Bus Minagawa Hinokuchi Line bound for “Yurarakukan” ** Get off at “Murahi Shrine Entrance” bus stop, 5-minute walk (few services) ** Get off at “Eda Clinic-mae” bus stop, 20-minute walk === Notes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book |Japanese |editor=Shigehide Ishihara {{ill|Shikinai Ronsha|lt=Shikinai Ronsha|ja|論社|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '栃木県栃木市岩舟町小野寺4697', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Murahi Shrine}} [[JA:村檜神社]] {{wikidata link|Q30935930}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Murahi Shrine |Image = [[File:Murahi shrine.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Tochigi Prefecture|Q44843|WD=Q44843|1=Tochigi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Tochigi City|Q44830|WD=Q44830|1=Tochigi (city)}} Iwafune-chō Onodera 4697 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} |Founded = Traditionally {{ill|lt=Taika|Q1077930|WD=Q1077930|1=Taika (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=646|Q33285|WD=Q33285|1=646}}) |Main hall = Three-bay {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}} |Alternate names = {{plainlist| * Onodera Hachiman{{Sfn|Tochigi Prefectural Education History Compilation Committee|1957|p=428}} * Murahi Hachiman{{Sfn|Tochigi City History Compilation Committee|1979|p=738}} }} |Pilgrimage = |Annual festival = |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Tochigi|label=Murahi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Murahi Shrine''' ('''Murahi Jinja'''; むらひじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|lt=Tochigi Prefecture|Q44843|WD=Q44843|1=Tochigi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Tochigi City|Q44830|WD=Q44830|1=Tochigi (city)}}. Under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a village shrine (gōsha). It claims the title of the Third Shrine of Shimotsuke Province. === History === According to shrine tradition, on {{ill|lt=Taika|Q1077930|WD=Q1077930|1=Taika (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=646|Q33285|WD=Q33285|1=646}}) {{ill|lt=September 29 (lunar calendar)|Q840263|WD=Q840263|lt=September 29|1=September 29 (Lunisolar)}}{{Sfn|Kazayama|1903|p=59}}, {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|lt=the Kumano deity|1=Kumano Gongen}} and {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054|lt=the Hie deity|1=Sannō Gongen}} ({{ill|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui no Kami|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}) were invited and enshrined{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}{{Sfn|Ishihara|1964|p=198}}. Ōyamakui no Kami is the deity of {{ill|lt=Mount Hiei|Q748040|WD=Q748040|1=Mount Hiei}}, and its enshrinement here has been noted in connection with the nearby {{ill|lt=Daijiji (Tochigi City)|Q11435473|WD=Q11435473|lt=Onodera-san Daijiji Temple|1=Daiji-ji Temple}}{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. The surrounding areas—{{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Onodera|UNKNOWN|lt=Onodera}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Kamioka|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamioka}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Shimo-oka|UNKNOWN|lt=Shimo-oka}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Mitani|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitani}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Niisato|UNKNOWN|lt=Niisato}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Furue|UNKNOWN|lt=Furue}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Shimotsuhara|Q109369623|WD=Q109369623|lt=Shimotsuhara|1=Shimotsuhara}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Tatamioka|UNKNOWN|lt=Tatamioka}}, Komaba (now part of {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Shizu|Q11662858|WD=Q11662858|lt=Shizu|1=Iwafunemachi Shizuka}}), and {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Washinosu|Q11676262|WD=Q11676262|lt=Washinosu|1=Iwafunemachi Washinosu}}—were collectively called the “Ten Villages of Onodera,” and the shrine is said to have been founded as their guardian shrine{{Sfn|Ishihara|1964|p=198}}. In {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daido|1=Daidō}} 2 ({{ill|lt=807|Q30016|WD=Q30016|1=807}}), the enshrined deity {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, originally worshiped at the Hachiman Shrine of Onoguchi in {{ill|lt=Minagawa Village|Q11580946|WD=Q11580946|1=Minagawa}}, which had been merged, was welcomed as the principal deity. Furthermore, during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243|1=Emperor Seiwa}} ({{ill|lt=858|Q30371|WD=Q30371|1=858}}–{{ill|lt=876|Q23815|WD=Q23815|1=876}}), the Hachiman deity that had been invited to Hachimanzawa in Minagawa Village was, during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōkō|Q440518|WD=Q440518|1=Emperor Kōkō}} ({{ill|lt=884|Q23895|WD=Q23895|1=884}}–{{ill|lt=887|Q25010|WD=Q25010|1=887}}), merged into Murahi Shrine on the grounds that its original site was impure{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}{{Sfn|Ishihara|1964|p=198}}. In {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340|1=Tengyō}} 2 ({{ill|lt=939|Q23422|WD=Q23422|1=939}}), {{ill|lt=Fujiwara no Hidesato|Q1136623|WD=Q1136623|1=Fujiwara no Hidesato}}, who went on to subdue {{ill|lt=Taira no Masakado|Q1207652|WD=Q1207652|1=Taira no Masakado}}, is said to have prayed here for victory{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. There is also a theory that the shrine itself was founded by Hidesato. In Kōno Morihiro’s *Shimotsuke Kokushi*, it is stated that Hidesato, as lord of {{ill|lt=Karasawayama Castle|Q11418602|WD=Q11418602|1=Karasawa Castle}}, founded Murahi Shrine about one *ri* away to the northeast (the demon gate) to protect the castle{{Sfn|Kōno|1893|p=12}}{{Sfn|Suzuka|1902|p=294}}. Some sources describe this as a “reconstruction”{{Sfn|Tochigi Prefectural Education History Compilation Committee|1957|p=428}}. From the medieval period onward, the shrine received devotion from the {{ill|lt=Shimotsuke Onodera clan|Q16141519|WD=Q16141519|1=Shimotsuke Onodera clan}} and the {{ill|lt=Sano clan|Q11385058|WD=Q11385058|1=Sano clan}}, lords of Karasawayama Castle{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. During the Edo period, it was also revered by merchants of Tochigi Town, where {{ill|lt=Tochigi-juku|Q30926264|WD=Q30926264|1=Tochigi-juku}} was established{{Sfn|Higano|1966|p=50}}. In front of the shrine buildings stands a pair of Oribe lanterns dedicated in {{ill|lt=Kyōhō|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 11, 12th month ({{ill|lt=1726|Q6936|WD=Q6936|1=1726}} December or January of the following year) by the Noguchi family, hemp wholesalers of “Tsukegi Kamimachi” (present-day {{ill|lt=Mannmachi (Tochigi City)|Q11353890|WD=Q11353890|lt=Mannmachi|1=Yorozuchō}} in Tochigi City){{Sfn|Tochigi City History Compilation Committee|1978|pp=527–528}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}), the shrine was designated a village shrine (gōsha) under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Records Compilation Office|1912|p=46}}. === Cultural Properties === === Important Cultural Property (National Designation) === * '''Main Hall''' : Built in {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|1=Tenbun}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1533|Q6443|WD=Q6443|1=1533}}){{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. According to some records, part of it was renovated in {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} 21 ({{ill|lt=1414|Q6179|WD=Q6179|1=1414}}), and a major renovation was carried out in Tenbun 2 by {{ill|lt=Sano Toyotsuna|Q11385105|WD=Q11385105|1=Sano Toyotsuna}}, lord of {{ill|lt=Sano Castle|Q11384983|WD=Q11384983|1=Sano Castle}}{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. A carving of a {{ill|lt=melon|Q5881191|WD=Q5881191|1=Melon}}, said to have been embedded by the master carpenter {{ill|lt=Hidari Jingorō|Q2668220|WD=Q2668220|1=Hidari Jingorō}} at the time of construction or major renovation, remains on the upper part of a side pillar on the southwest side of the hall{{Sfn|Kazayama|1903|p=59}}{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. : It is a three-bay Kasuga-zukuri structure with a cypress-bark roof{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. There is no distinction between the worship hall and the main hall{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. The {{ill|lt=bracket complexes|Q7814068|WD=Q7814068|lt=to-gumi|1=Tokyō (architecture)}} above the pillars supporting the front eaves are noted for unique features not seen in other shrine buildings of the mid-to-late Muromachi period, which was key to its cultural property designation{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. : On {{ill|lt=August 1|Q2788|WD=Q2788|1=August 1}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 41), it was designated a Specially Protected Building under the then {{ill|lt=Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law|Q11411552|WD=Q11411552|1=Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law}} (equivalent to an {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} under the {{ill|lt=Law for the Protection of Cultural Properties|Q1188502|WD=Q1188502|1=Cultural Properties Protection Law}})Home Ministry Notification No. 76 of August 1, Meiji 41 (see: [{{NDLDC|2950877}} National Diet Library Digital Collection]). === Tochigi City Designated Natural Monument === * '''Shrine Grove''' : Designated as a natural monument of {{ill|lt=Iwafune Town|Q1203561|WD=Q1203561|1=Iwafune, Tochigi}} on {{ill|lt=April 1|Q2510|WD=Q2510|1=April 1}}, {{ill|lt=1972|Q2476|WD=Q2476|1=1972}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 47){{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}. Following the municipal merger on {{ill|lt=April 5|Q2507|WD=Q2507|1=April 5}}, {{ill|lt=2014|Q1999|WD=Q1999|1=2014}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 26), it was inherited by Tochigi City{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}. === Grounds === File:Murahi-jinja Main Hall.JPG|Main hall File:Murahi-jinja Main Hall tablet.JPG|Plaque of the worship hall File:Murahi-jinja Torii.JPG|Torii gate File:Murahi-jinja Kagura Hall.JPG|Kagura hall File:Murahi-jinja Shrine office.JPG|Shrine gate File:Murahi-jinja jukuyo-tou.JPG|Jūkyoya-tō stone monument === Subsidiary Shrines === Of the 11 subsidiary shrines, 8 are enshrined together in the subordinate shrine building of Nishinomiya Shrine. Orihime Shrine was destroyed by fire, and Itsukushima Shrine collapsed and has not been rebuilt. * Nishinomiya Shrine * Sumiyoshi Shrine * Raiden Shrine / Atago Shrine File:Murahi-jinja Nishinomiya-jinja.JPG|Nishinomiya Shrine torii File:Murahi-jinja Nishinomiya-jinja.JPG Main Hall.JPG|Nishinomiya Shrine hall File:村檜神社 西宮神社 末社.jpg|Nishinomiya Shrine subordinate shrines File:Murahi-jinja Atago-jinja.JPG|Raiden Shrine / Atago Shrine File:Murahi-jinja Shumiyoshi-jinja.JPG|Sumiyoshi Shrine === Access === * 15 minutes by car from {{ill|lt=Iwafune Station|Q11475586|WD=Q11475586|1=Iwafune Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Ryōmō Line|Q253391|WD=Q253391|lt=JR Ryōmō Line|1=Ryōmō Line}} * About 25 minutes by car from {{ill|lt=Sano-Fujioka Interchange|Q10885981|WD=Q10885981|lt=Sano-Fujioka I.C.|1=Sano-Fujioka interchange}} on the {{ill|lt=Tōhoku Expressway|Q428428|WD=Q428428|1=Tōhoku Expressway}} * 40 minutes from Tochigi Station on the JR Ryōmō Line / {{ill|lt=Tōbu Nikkō Line|Q845954|WD=Q845954|1=Tōbu Nikkō Line}} via the Tochigi City Fureai Bus Minagawa Hinokuchi Line bound for “Yurarakukan” ** Get off at “Murahi Shrine Entrance” bus stop, 5-minute walk (few services) ** Get off at “Eda Clinic-mae” bus stop, 20-minute walk === Notes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book |Japanese |editor=Shigehide Ishihara |date=1964-02-11 |title=Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Gazetteer |publisher=Tochigi Prefectural Shrine Association |ref={{SfnRef|Ishihara|1964}} }} * {{Cite book |Japanese |editor=Iwafune Town Board of Education |date=1974-03-25 |title=History of Iwafune Town |publisher=Iwafune Town |ref={{SfnRef|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974}} }} * {{Cite book |Japanese |editor=Hiroo Kazayama |date=1903-04-20 |title=History of Shrines in Shimotsuke: Volume 4 |publisher=Hiroo Kazayama |page=59 |ref={{SfnRef|Kazayama|1903}} }}{{National Diet Library Digital Collection|815435/1/61|format=NDLJP}} * {{Cite book |Japanese |editor=Morihiro Kōno |date=1893-10 |title=Shimotsuke Kokushi: Vol. 3 |publisher=Satō San’ichirō |page=12 |ref={{SfnRef|Kōno|1893}} }}{{National Diet Library Digital Collection|763928/1/13|format=NDLJP}} * {{Cite book |Japanese |author={{ill|lt=Ren’in Suzuka|Q6536446|WD=Q6536446|1=Suzuka Tsuratane}} (compiler) {{ill|Shikinai Ronsha|lt=Shikinai Ronsha|ja|論社|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '栃木県栃木市岩舟町小野寺4697', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Murahi Shrine}} [[JA:村檜神社]] {{wikidata link|Q30935930}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Murahi Shrine |Image = [[File:Murahi shrine.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Tochigi Prefecture|Q44843|WD=Q44843|1=Tochigi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Tochigi City|Q44830|WD=Q44830|1=Tochigi (city)}} Iwafune-chō Onodera 4697 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} |Founded = Traditionally {{ill|lt=Taika|Q1077930|WD=Q1077930|1=Taika (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=646|Q33285|WD=Q33285|1=646}}) |Main hall = Three-bay {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}} |Alternate names = {{plainlist| * Onodera Hachiman{{Sfn|Tochigi Prefectural Education History Compilation Committee|1957|p=428}} * Murahi Hachiman{{Sfn|Tochigi City History Compilation Committee|1979|p=738}} }} |Pilgrimage = |Annual festival = |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Tochigi|label=Murahi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Murahi Shrine''' ('''Murahi Jinja'''; むらひじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|lt=Tochigi Prefecture|Q44843|WD=Q44843|1=Tochigi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Tochigi City|Q44830|WD=Q44830|1=Tochigi (city)}}. Under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a village shrine (gōsha). It claims the title of the Third Shrine of Shimotsuke Province. === History === According to shrine tradition, on {{ill|lt=Taika|Q1077930|WD=Q1077930|1=Taika (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=646|Q33285|WD=Q33285|1=646}}) {{ill|lt=September 29 (lunar calendar)|Q840263|WD=Q840263|lt=September 29|1=September 29 (Lunisolar)}}{{Sfn|Kazayama|1903|p=59}}, {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|lt=the Kumano deity|1=Kumano Gongen}} and {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054|lt=the Hie deity|1=Sannō Gongen}} ({{ill|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui no Kami|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}) were invited and enshrined{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}{{Sfn|Ishihara|1964|p=198}}. Ōyamakui no Kami is the deity of {{ill|lt=Mount Hiei|Q748040|WD=Q748040|1=Mount Hiei}}, and its enshrinement here has been noted in connection with the nearby {{ill|lt=Daijiji (Tochigi City)|Q11435473|WD=Q11435473|lt=Onodera-san Daijiji Temple|1=Daiji-ji Temple}}{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. The surrounding areas—{{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Onodera|UNKNOWN|lt=Onodera}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Kamioka|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamioka}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Shimo-oka|UNKNOWN|lt=Shimo-oka}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Mitani|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitani}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Niisato|UNKNOWN|lt=Niisato}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Furue|UNKNOWN|lt=Furue}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Shimotsuhara|Q109369623|WD=Q109369623|lt=Shimotsuhara|1=Shimotsuhara}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Tatamioka|UNKNOWN|lt=Tatamioka}}, Komaba (now part of {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Shizu|Q11662858|WD=Q11662858|lt=Shizu|1=Iwafunemachi Shizuka}}), and {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Washinosu|Q11676262|WD=Q11676262|lt=Washinosu|1=Iwafunemachi Washinosu}}—were collectively called the “Ten Villages of Onodera,” and the shrine is said to have been founded as their guardian shrine{{Sfn|Ishihara|1964|p=198}}. In {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daido|1=Daidō}} 2 ({{ill|lt=807|Q30016|WD=Q30016|1=807}}), the enshrined deity {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, originally worshiped at the Hachiman Shrine of Onoguchi in {{ill|lt=Minagawa Village|Q11580946|WD=Q11580946|1=Minagawa}}, which had been merged, was welcomed as the principal deity. Furthermore, during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243|1=Emperor Seiwa}} ({{ill|lt=858|Q30371|WD=Q30371|1=858}}–{{ill|lt=876|Q23815|WD=Q23815|1=876}}), the Hachiman deity that had been invited to Hachimanzawa in Minagawa Village was, during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōkō|Q440518|WD=Q440518|1=Emperor Kōkō}} ({{ill|lt=884|Q23895|WD=Q23895|1=884}}–{{ill|lt=887|Q25010|WD=Q25010|1=887}}), merged into Murahi Shrine on the grounds that its original site was impure{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}{{Sfn|Ishihara|1964|p=198}}. In {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340|1=Tengyō}} 2 ({{ill|lt=939|Q23422|WD=Q23422|1=939}}), {{ill|lt=Fujiwara no Hidesato|Q1136623|WD=Q1136623|1=Fujiwara no Hidesato}}, who went on to subdue {{ill|lt=Taira no Masakado|Q1207652|WD=Q1207652|1=Taira no Masakado}}, is said to have prayed here for victory{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. There is also a theory that the shrine itself was founded by Hidesato. In Kōno Morihiro’s *Shimotsuke Kokushi*, it is stated that Hidesato, as lord of {{ill|lt=Karasawayama Castle|Q11418602|WD=Q11418602|1=Karasawa Castle}}, founded Murahi Shrine about one *ri* away to the northeast (the demon gate) to protect the castle{{Sfn|Kōno|1893|p=12}}{{Sfn|Suzuka|1902|p=294}}. Some sources describe this as a “reconstruction”{{Sfn|Tochigi Prefectural Education History Compilation Committee|1957|p=428}}. From the medieval period onward, the shrine received devotion from the {{ill|lt=Shimotsuke Onodera clan|Q16141519|WD=Q16141519|1=Shimotsuke Onodera clan}} and the {{ill|lt=Sano clan|Q11385058|WD=Q11385058|1=Sano clan}}, lords of Karasawayama Castle{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. During the Edo period, it was also revered by merchants of Tochigi Town, where {{ill|lt=Tochigi-juku|Q30926264|WD=Q30926264|1=Tochigi-juku}} was established{{Sfn|Higano|1966|p=50}}. In front of the shrine buildings stands a pair of Oribe lanterns dedicated in {{ill|lt=Kyōhō|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 11, 12th month ({{ill|lt=1726|Q6936|WD=Q6936|1=1726}} December or January of the following year) by the Noguchi family, hemp wholesalers of “Tsukegi Kamimachi” (present-day {{ill|lt=Mannmachi (Tochigi City)|Q11353890|WD=Q11353890|lt=Mannmachi|1=Yorozuchō}} in Tochigi City){{Sfn|Tochigi City History Compilation Committee|1978|pp=527–528}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}), the shrine was designated a village shrine (gōsha) under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Records Compilation Office|1912|p=46}}. === Cultural Properties === === Important Cultural Property (National Designation) === * '''Main Hall''' : Built in {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|1=Tenbun}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1533|Q6443|WD=Q6443|1=1533}}){{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. According to some records, part of it was renovated in {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} 21 ({{ill|lt=1414|Q6179|WD=Q6179|1=1414}}), and a major renovation was carried out in Tenbun 2 by {{ill|lt=Sano Toyotsuna|Q11385105|WD=Q11385105|1=Sano Toyotsuna}}, lord of {{ill|lt=Sano Castle|Q11384983|WD=Q11384983|1=Sano Castle}}{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. A carving of a {{ill|lt=melon|Q5881191|WD=Q5881191|1=Melon}}, said to have been embedded by the master carpenter {{ill|lt=Hidari Jingorō|Q2668220|WD=Q2668220|1=Hidari Jingorō}} at the time of construction or major renovation, remains on the upper part of a side pillar on the southwest side of the hall{{Sfn|Kazayama|1903|p=59}}{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. : It is a three-bay Kasuga-zukuri structure with a cypress-bark roof{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. There is no distinction between the worship hall and the main hall{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. The {{ill|lt=bracket complexes|Q7814068|WD=Q7814068|lt=to-gumi|1=Tokyō (architecture)}} above the pillars supporting the front eaves are noted for unique features not seen in other shrine buildings of the mid-to-late Muromachi period, which was key to its cultural property designation{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. : On {{ill|lt=August 1|Q2788|WD=Q2788|1=August 1}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 41), it was designated a Specially Protected Building under the then {{ill|lt=Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law|Q11411552|WD=Q11411552|1=Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law}} (equivalent to an {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} under the {{ill|lt=Law for the Protection of Cultural Properties|Q1188502|WD=Q1188502|1=Cultural Properties Protection Law}})Home Ministry Notification No. 76 of August 1, Meiji 41 (see: [{{NDLDC|2950877}} National Diet Library Digital Collection]). === Tochigi City Designated Natural Monument === * '''Shrine Grove''' : Designated as a natural monument of {{ill|lt=Iwafune Town|Q1203561|WD=Q1203561|1=Iwafune, Tochigi}} on {{ill|lt=April 1|Q2510|WD=Q2510|1=April 1}}, {{ill|lt=1972|Q2476|WD=Q2476|1=1972}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 47){{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}. Following the municipal merger on {{ill|lt=April 5|Q2507|WD=Q2507|1=April 5}}, {{ill|lt=2014|Q1999|WD=Q1999|1=2014}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 26), it was inherited by Tochigi City{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}. === Grounds === File:Murahi-jinja Main Hall.JPG|Main hall File:Murahi-jinja Main Hall tablet.JPG|Plaque of the worship hall File:Murahi-jinja Torii.JPG|Torii gate File:Murahi-jinja Kagura Hall.JPG|Kagura hall File:Murahi-jinja Shrine office.JPG|Shrine gate File:Murahi-jinja jukuyo-tou.JPG|Jūkyoya-tō stone monument === Subsidiary Shrines === Of the 11 subsidiary shrines, 8 are enshrined together in the subordinate shrine building of Nishinomiya Shrine. Orihime Shrine was destroyed by fire, and Itsukushima Shrine collapsed and has not been rebuilt. * Nishinomiya Shrine * Sumiyoshi Shrine * Raiden Shrine / Atago Shrine File:Murahi-jinja File:Murahi-jinja Atago-jinja.JPG|Raiden Shrine / Atago Shrine File:Murahi-jinja Shumiyoshi-jinja.JPG|Sumiyoshi Shrine === Access === * 15 minutes by car from {{ill|lt=Iwafune Shrine}} [[JA:村檜神社]] {{wikidata link|Q30935930}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Murahi Shrine |Image = [[File:Murahi shrine.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Tochigi Prefecture|Q44843|WD=Q44843|1=Tochigi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Tochigi City|Q44830|WD=Q44830|1=Tochigi (city)}} Iwafune-chō Onodera 4697 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} |Founded = Traditionally {{ill|lt=Taika|Q1077930|WD=Q1077930|1=Taika (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=646|Q33285|WD=Q33285|1=646}}) |Main hall = Three-bay {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}} |Alternate names = {{plainlist| * Onodera Hachiman{{Sfn|Tochigi Prefectural Education History Compilation Committee|1957|p=428}} * Murahi Hachiman{{Sfn|Tochigi City History Compilation Committee|1979|p=738}} }} |Pilgrimage = |Annual festival = |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Tochigi|label=Murahi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Murahi Shrine''' ('''Murahi Jinja'''; むらひじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|lt=Tochigi Prefecture|Q44843|WD=Q44843|1=Tochigi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Tochigi City|Q44830|WD=Q44830|1=Tochigi (city)}}. Under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a village shrine (gōsha). It claims the title of the Third Shrine of Shimotsuke Province. === History === According to shrine tradition, on {{ill|lt=Taika|Q1077930|WD=Q1077930|1=Taika (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=646|Q33285|WD=Q33285|1=646}}) {{ill|lt=September 29 (lunar calendar)|Q840263|WD=Q840263|lt=September 29|1=September 29 (Lunisolar)}}{{Sfn|Kazayama|1903|p=59}}, {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|lt=the Kumano deity|1=Kumano Gongen}} and {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054|lt=the Hie deity|1=Sannō Gongen}} ({{ill|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui no Kami|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}) were invited and enshrined{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}{{Sfn|Ishihara|1964|p=198}}. Ōyamakui no Kami is the deity of {{ill|lt=Mount Hiei|Q748040|WD=Q748040|1=Mount Hiei}}, and its enshrinement here has been noted in connection with the nearby {{ill|lt=Daijiji (Tochigi City)|Q11435473|WD=Q11435473|lt=Onodera-san Daijiji Temple|1=Daiji-ji Temple}}{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. The surrounding areas—{{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Onodera|UNKNOWN|lt=Onodera}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Kamioka|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamioka}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Shimo-oka|UNKNOWN|lt=Shimo-oka}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Mitani|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitani}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Niisato|UNKNOWN|lt=Niisato}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Furue|UNKNOWN|lt=Furue}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Shimotsuhara|Q109369623|WD=Q109369623|lt=Shimotsuhara|1=Shimotsuhara}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Tatamioka|UNKNOWN|lt=Tatamioka}}, Komaba (now part of {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Shizu|Q11662858|WD=Q11662858|lt=Shizu|1=Iwafunemachi Shizuka}}), and {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Washinosu|Q11676262|WD=Q11676262|lt=Washinosu|1=Iwafunemachi Washinosu}}—were collectively called the “Ten Villages of Onodera,” and the shrine is said to have been founded as their guardian shrine{{Sfn|Ishihara|1964|p=198}}. In {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daido|1=Daidō}} 2 ({{ill|lt=807|Q30016|WD=Q30016|1=807}}), the enshrined deity {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, originally worshiped at the Hachiman Shrine of Onoguchi in {{ill|lt=Minagawa Village|Q11580946|WD=Q11580946|1=Minagawa}}, which had been merged, was welcomed as the principal deity. Furthermore, during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243|1=Emperor Seiwa}} ({{ill|lt=858|Q30371|WD=Q30371|1=858}}–{{ill|lt=876|Q23815|WD=Q23815|1=876}}), the Hachiman deity that had been invited to Hachimanzawa in Minagawa Village was, during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōkō|Q440518|WD=Q440518|1=Emperor Kōkō}} ({{ill|lt=884|Q23895|WD=Q23895|1=884}}–{{ill|lt=887|Q25010|WD=Q25010|1=887}}), merged into Murahi Shrine on the grounds that its original site was impure{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}{{Sfn|Ishihara|1964|p=198}}. In {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340|1=Tengyō}} 2 ({{ill|lt=939|Q23422|WD=Q23422|1=939}}), {{ill|lt=Fujiwara no Hidesato|Q1136623|WD=Q1136623|1=Fujiwara no Hidesato}}, who went on to subdue {{ill|lt=Taira no Masakado|Q1207652|WD=Q1207652|1=Taira no Masakado}}, is said to have prayed here for victory{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. There is also a theory that the shrine itself was founded by Hidesato. In Kōno Morihiro’s *Shimotsuke Kokushi*, it is stated that Hidesato, as lord of {{ill|lt=Karasawayama Castle|Q11418602|WD=Q11418602|1=Karasawa Castle}}, founded Murahi Shrine about one *ri* away to the northeast (the demon gate) to protect the castle{{Sfn|Kōno|1893|p=12}}{{Sfn|Suzuka|1902|p=294}}. Some sources describe this as a “reconstruction”{{Sfn|Tochigi Prefectural Education History Compilation Committee|1957|p=428}}. From the medieval period onward, the shrine received devotion from the {{ill|lt=Shimotsuke Onodera clan|Q16141519|WD=Q16141519|1=Shimotsuke Onodera clan}} and the {{ill|lt=Sano clan|Q11385058|WD=Q11385058|1=Sano clan}}, lords of Karasawayama Castle{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. During the Edo period, it was also revered by merchants of Tochigi Town, where {{ill|lt=Tochigi-juku|Q30926264|WD=Q30926264|1=Tochigi-juku}} was established{{Sfn|Higano|1966|p=50}}. In front of the shrine buildings stands a pair of Oribe lanterns dedicated in {{ill|lt=Kyōhō|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 11, 12th month ({{ill|lt=1726|Q6936|WD=Q6936|1=1726}} December or January of the following year) by the Noguchi family, hemp wholesalers of “Tsukegi Kamimachi” (present-day {{ill|lt=Mannmachi (Tochigi City)|Q11353890|WD=Q11353890|lt=Mannmachi|1=Yorozuchō}} in Tochigi City){{Sfn|Tochigi City History Compilation Committee|1978|pp=527–528}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}), the shrine was designated a village shrine (gōsha) under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Records Compilation Office|1912|p=46}}. === Cultural Properties === === Important Cultural Property (National Designation) === * '''Main Hall''' : Built in {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|1=Tenbun}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1533|Q6443|WD=Q6443|1=1533}}){{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. According to some records, part of it was renovated in {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} 21 ({{ill|lt=1414|Q6179|WD=Q6179|1=1414}}), and a major renovation was carried out in Tenbun 2 by {{ill|lt=Sano Toyotsuna|Q11385105|WD=Q11385105|1=Sano Toyotsuna}}, lord of {{ill|lt=Sano Castle|Q11384983|WD=Q11384983|1=Sano Castle}}{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. A carving of a {{ill|lt=melon|Q5881191|WD=Q5881191|1=Melon}}, said to have been embedded by the master carpenter {{ill|lt=Hidari Jingorō|Q2668220|WD=Q2668220|1=Hidari Jingorō}} at the time of construction or major renovation, remains on the upper part of a side pillar on the southwest side of the hall{{Sfn|Kazayama|1903|p=59}}{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. : It is a three-bay Kasuga-zukuri structure with a cypress-bark roof{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. There is no distinction between the worship hall and the main hall{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. The {{ill|lt=bracket complexes|Q7814068|WD=Q7814068|lt=to-gumi|1=Tokyō (architecture)}} above the pillars supporting the front eaves are noted for unique features not seen in other shrine buildings of the mid-to-late Muromachi period, which was key to its cultural property designation{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. : On {{ill|lt=August 1|Q2788|WD=Q2788|1=August 1}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 41), it was designated a Specially Protected Building under the then {{ill|lt=Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law|Q11411552|WD=Q11411552|1=Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law}} (equivalent to an {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} under the {{ill|lt=Law for the Protection of Cultural Properties|Q1188502|WD=Q1188502|1=Cultural Properties Protection Law}})Home Ministry Notification No. 76 of August 1, Meiji 41 (see: [{{NDLDC|2950877}} National Diet Library Digital Collection]). === Tochigi City Designated Natural Monument === * '''Shrine Grove''' : Designated as a natural monument of {{ill|lt=Iwafune Town|Q1203561|WD=Q1203561|1=Iwafune, Tochigi}} on {{ill|lt=April 1|Q2510|WD=Q2510|1=April 1}}, {{ill|lt=1972|Q2476|WD=Q2476|1=1972}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 47){{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}. Following the municipal merger on {{ill|lt=April 5|Q2507|WD=Q2507|1=April 5}}, {{ill|lt=2014|Q1999|WD=Q1999|1=2014}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 26), it was inherited by Tochigi City{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}. === Grounds === File:Murahi-jinja Main Hall.JPG|Main hall File:Murahi-jinja Main Hall tablet.JPG|Plaque of the worship hall File:Murahi-jinja Torii.JPG|Torii gate File:Murahi-jinja Kagura Hall.JPG|Kagura hall File:Murahi-jinja Shrine office.JPG|Shrine gate File:Murahi-jinja jukuyo-tou.JPG|Jūkyoya-tō stone monument === Subsidiary Shrines === Of the 11 subsidiary shrines, 8 are enshrined together in the subordinate shrine building of Nishinomiya Shrine. Orihime Shrine was destroyed by fire, and Itsukushima Shrine collapsed and has not been rebuilt. * Nishinomiya Shrine * Sumiyoshi Shrine * Raiden Shrine / Atago Shrine File:Murahi-jinja Nishinomiya-jinja.JPG|Nishinomiya Shrine torii File:Murahi-jinja Nishinomiya-jinja.JPG Main Hall.JPG|Nishinomiya Shrine hall File:村檜神社 西宮神社 末社.jpg|Nishinomiya Shrine subordinate shrines File:Murahi-jinja Atago-jinja.JPG|Raiden Shrine / Atago Shrine File:Murahi-jinja Shumiyoshi-jinja.JPG|Sumiyoshi Shrine === Access === * 15 minutes by car from {{ill|lt=Iwafune Station|Q11475586|WD=Q11475586|1=Iwafune Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Ryōmō Line|Q253391|WD=Q253391|lt=JR Ryōmō Line|1=Ryōmō Line}} * About 25 minutes by car from {{ill|lt=Sano-Fujioka Interchange|Q10885981|WD=Q10885981|lt=Sano-Fujioka I.C.|1=Sano-Fujioka interchange}} on the {{ill|lt=Tōhoku Expressway|Q428428|WD=Q428428|1=Tōhoku Expressway}} * 40 minutes from Tochigi Station on the JR Ryōmō Line / {{ill|lt=Tōbu Nikkō Line|Q845954|WD=Q845954|1=Tōbu Nikkō Line}} via the Tochigi City Fureai Bus Minagawa Hinokuchi Line bound for “Yurarakukan” ** Get off at “Murahi Shrine Entrance” bus stop, 5-minute walk (few services) ** Get off at “Eda Clinic-mae” bus stop, 20-minute walk === Notes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book |Japanese |editor=Shigehide Ishihara |date=1964-02-11 |title=Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Gazetteer |publisher=Tochigi Prefectural Shrine Association |ref={{SfnRef|Ishihara|1964}} }} * {{Cite book |Japanese |editor=Iwafune Town Board of Education |date=1974-03-25 |title=History of Iwafune Town |publisher=Iwafune Town |ref={{SfnRef|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974}} }} * {{Cite book |Japanese |editor=Hiroo Kazayama |date=1903-04-20 |title=History of Shrines in Shimotsuke: Volume 4 |publisher=Hiroo Kazayama |page=59 |ref={{SfnRef|Kazayama|1903}} }}{{National Diet Library Digital Collection|815435/1/61|format=NDLJP}} * {{Cite book |Japanese |editor=Morihiro Kōno |date=1893-10 |title=Shimotsuke Kokushi: Vol. 3 |publisher=Satō San’ichirō |page=12 |ref={{SfnRef|Kōno|1893}} }}{{National Diet Library Digital Collection|763928/1/13|format=NDLJP}} * {{Cite book |Japanese |author={{ill|lt=Ren’in Suzuka|Q6536446|WD=Q6536446|1=Suzuka Tsuratane}} (compiler) (P3225) == * 9060005005955 == Commons category (P373) == * Murahi Shrine == said to be the same as (P460) == * {{ill|Q135039971|ltq=Q135039971|WD=Q135039971|1=Murahino Shrine}} ** P2868: {{ill|Shikinai Ronsha|lt=Shikinai Ronsha|ja|論社|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '栃木県栃木市岩舟町小野寺4697', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Murahi Shrine}} [[JA:村檜神社]] {{wikidata link|Q30935930}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Murahi Shrine |Image = [[File:Murahi shrine.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Tochigi Prefecture|Q44843|WD=Q44843|1=Tochigi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Tochigi City|Q44830|WD=Q44830|1=Tochigi (city)}} Iwafune-chō Onodera 4697 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} |Founded = Traditionally {{ill|lt=Taika|Q1077930|WD=Q1077930|1=Taika (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=646|Q33285|WD=Q33285|1=646}}) |Main hall = Three-bay {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}} |Alternate names = {{plainlist| * Onodera Hachiman{{Sfn|Tochigi Prefectural Education History Compilation Committee|1957|p=428}} * Murahi Hachiman{{Sfn|Tochigi City History Compilation Committee|1979|p=738}} }} |Pilgrimage = |Annual festival = |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Tochigi|label=Murahi Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Murahi Shrine''' ('''Murahi Jinja'''; むらひじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|lt=Tochigi Prefecture|Q44843|WD=Q44843|1=Tochigi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Tochigi City|Q44830|WD=Q44830|1=Tochigi (city)}}. Under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a village shrine (gōsha). It claims the title of the Third Shrine of Shimotsuke Province. === History === According to shrine tradition, on {{ill|lt=Taika|Q1077930|WD=Q1077930|1=Taika (era)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=646|Q33285|WD=Q33285|1=646}}) {{ill|lt=September 29 (lunar calendar)|Q840263|WD=Q840263|lt=September 29|1=September 29 (Lunisolar)}}{{Sfn|Kazayama|1903|p=59}}, {{ill|lt=Kumano Gongen|Q11568925|WD=Q11568925|lt=the Kumano deity|1=Kumano Gongen}} and {{ill|lt=Sannō Gongen|Q11470054|WD=Q11470054|lt=the Hie deity|1=Sannō Gongen}} ({{ill|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui no Kami|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}) were invited and enshrined{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}{{Sfn|Ishihara|1964|p=198}}. Ōyamakui no Kami is the deity of {{ill|lt=Mount Hiei|Q748040|WD=Q748040|1=Mount Hiei}}, and its enshrinement here has been noted in connection with the nearby {{ill|lt=Daijiji (Tochigi City)|Q11435473|WD=Q11435473|lt=Onodera-san Daijiji Temple|1=Daiji-ji Temple}}{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. The surrounding areas—{{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Onodera|UNKNOWN|lt=Onodera}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Kamioka|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamioka}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Shimo-oka|UNKNOWN|lt=Shimo-oka}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Mitani|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitani}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Niisato|UNKNOWN|lt=Niisato}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Furue|UNKNOWN|lt=Furue}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Shimotsuhara|Q109369623|WD=Q109369623|lt=Shimotsuhara|1=Shimotsuhara}}, {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Tatamioka|UNKNOWN|lt=Tatamioka}}, Komaba (now part of {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Shizu|Q11662858|WD=Q11662858|lt=Shizu|1=Iwafunemachi Shizuka}}), and {{ill|lt=Iwafune-chō Washinosu|Q11676262|WD=Q11676262|lt=Washinosu|1=Iwafunemachi Washinosu}}—were collectively called the “Ten Villages of Onodera,” and the shrine is said to have been founded as their guardian shrine{{Sfn|Ishihara|1964|p=198}}. In {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|WD=Q1373191|lt=Daido|1=Daidō}} 2 ({{ill|lt=807|Q30016|WD=Q30016|1=807}}), the enshrined deity {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, originally worshiped at the Hachiman Shrine of Onoguchi in {{ill|lt=Minagawa Village|Q11580946|WD=Q11580946|1=Minagawa}}, which had been merged, was welcomed as the principal deity. Furthermore, during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|WD=Q349243|1=Emperor Seiwa}} ({{ill|lt=858|Q30371|WD=Q30371|1=858}}–{{ill|lt=876|Q23815|WD=Q23815|1=876}}), the Hachiman deity that had been invited to Hachimanzawa in Minagawa Village was, during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōkō|Q440518|WD=Q440518|1=Emperor Kōkō}} ({{ill|lt=884|Q23895|WD=Q23895|1=884}}–{{ill|lt=887|Q25010|WD=Q25010|1=887}}), merged into Murahi Shrine on the grounds that its original site was impure{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}{{Sfn|Ishihara|1964|p=198}}. In {{ill|lt=Tengyō|Q1327340|WD=Q1327340|1=Tengyō}} 2 ({{ill|lt=939|Q23422|WD=Q23422|1=939}}), {{ill|lt=Fujiwara no Hidesato|Q1136623|WD=Q1136623|1=Fujiwara no Hidesato}}, who went on to subdue {{ill|lt=Taira no Masakado|Q1207652|WD=Q1207652|1=Taira no Masakado}}, is said to have prayed here for victory{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. There is also a theory that the shrine itself was founded by Hidesato. In Kōno Morihiro’s *Shimotsuke Kokushi*, it is stated that Hidesato, as lord of {{ill|lt=Karasawayama Castle|Q11418602|WD=Q11418602|1=Karasawa Castle}}, founded Murahi Shrine about one *ri* away to the northeast (the demon gate) to protect the castle{{Sfn|Kōno|1893|p=12}}{{Sfn|Suzuka|1902|p=294}}. Some sources describe this as a “reconstruction”{{Sfn|Tochigi Prefectural Education History Compilation Committee|1957|p=428}}. From the medieval period onward, the shrine received devotion from the {{ill|lt=Shimotsuke Onodera clan|Q16141519|WD=Q16141519|1=Shimotsuke Onodera clan}} and the {{ill|lt=Sano clan|Q11385058|WD=Q11385058|1=Sano clan}}, lords of Karasawayama Castle{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. During the Edo period, it was also revered by merchants of Tochigi Town, where {{ill|lt=Tochigi-juku|Q30926264|WD=Q30926264|1=Tochigi-juku}} was established{{Sfn|Higano|1966|p=50}}. In front of the shrine buildings stands a pair of Oribe lanterns dedicated in {{ill|lt=Kyōhō|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 11, 12th month ({{ill|lt=1726|Q6936|WD=Q6936|1=1726}} December or January of the following year) by the Noguchi family, hemp wholesalers of “Tsukegi Kamimachi” (present-day {{ill|lt=Mannmachi (Tochigi City)|Q11353890|WD=Q11353890|lt=Mannmachi|1=Yorozuchō}} in Tochigi City){{Sfn|Tochigi City History Compilation Committee|1978|pp=527–528}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}), the shrine was designated a village shrine (gōsha) under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Records Compilation Office|1912|p=46}}. === Cultural Properties === === Important Cultural Property (National Designation) === * '''Main Hall''' : Built in {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|1=Tenbun}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1533|Q6443|WD=Q6443|1=1533}}){{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. According to some records, part of it was renovated in {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} 21 ({{ill|lt=1414|Q6179|WD=Q6179|1=1414}}), and a major renovation was carried out in Tenbun 2 by {{ill|lt=Sano Toyotsuna|Q11385105|WD=Q11385105|1=Sano Toyotsuna}}, lord of {{ill|lt=Sano Castle|Q11384983|WD=Q11384983|1=Sano Castle}}{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. A carving of a {{ill|lt=melon|Q5881191|WD=Q5881191|1=Melon}}, said to have been embedded by the master carpenter {{ill|lt=Hidari Jingorō|Q2668220|WD=Q2668220|1=Hidari Jingorō}} at the time of construction or major renovation, remains on the upper part of a side pillar on the southwest side of the hall{{Sfn|Kazayama|1903|p=59}}{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. : It is a three-bay Kasuga-zukuri structure with a cypress-bark roof{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}{{Sfn|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974|p=14}}. There is no distinction between the worship hall and the main hall{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. The {{ill|lt=bracket complexes|Q7814068|WD=Q7814068|lt=to-gumi|1=Tokyō (architecture)}} above the pillars supporting the front eaves are noted for unique features not seen in other shrine buildings of the mid-to-late Muromachi period, which was key to its cultural property designation{{Sfn|Murakami|1967|p=121}}. : On {{ill|lt=August 1|Q2788|WD=Q2788|1=August 1}}, {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 41), it was designated a Specially Protected Building under the then {{ill|lt=Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Law|Q11411552|WD=Q11411552|1=Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law}} (equivalent to an {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} under the {{ill|lt=Law for the Protection of Cultural Properties|Q1188502|WD=Q1188502|1=Cultural Properties Protection Law}})Home Ministry Notification No. 76 of August 1, Meiji 41 (see: [{{NDLDC|2950877}} National Diet Library Digital Collection]). === Tochigi City Designated Natural Monument === * '''Shrine Grove''' : Designated as a natural monument of {{ill|lt=Iwafune Town|Q1203561|WD=Q1203561|1=Iwafune, Tochigi}} on {{ill|lt=April 1|Q2510|WD=Q2510|1=April 1}}, {{ill|lt=1972|Q2476|WD=Q2476|1=1972}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 47){{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}. Following the municipal merger on {{ill|lt=April 5|Q2507|WD=Q2507|1=April 5}}, {{ill|lt=2014|Q1999|WD=Q1999|1=2014}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 26), it was inherited by Tochigi City{{Sfn|Tochigi City Board of Education|2014|p=21}}. === Grounds === File:Murahi-jinja Main Hall.JPG|Main hall File:Murahi-jinja Main Hall tablet.JPG|Plaque of the worship hall File:Murahi-jinja Torii.JPG|Torii gate File:Murahi-jinja Kagura Hall.JPG|Kagura hall File:Murahi-jinja Shrine office.JPG|Shrine gate File:Murahi-jinja jukuyo-tou.JPG|Jūkyoya-tō stone monument === Subsidiary Shrines === Of the 11 subsidiary shrines, 8 are enshrined together in the subordinate shrine building of Nishinomiya Shrine. Orihime Shrine was destroyed by fire, and Itsukushima Shrine collapsed and has not been rebuilt. * Nishinomiya Shrine * Sumiyoshi Shrine * Raiden Shrine / Atago Shrine File:Murahi-jinja Nishinomiya-jinja.JPG|Nishinomiya Shrine torii File:Murahi-jinja Nishinomiya-jinja.JPG Main Hall.JPG|Nishinomiya Shrine hall File:村檜神社 西宮神社 末社.jpg|Nishinomiya Shrine subordinate shrines File:Murahi-jinja Atago-jinja.JPG|Raiden Shrine / Atago Shrine File:Murahi-jinja Shumiyoshi-jinja.JPG|Sumiyoshi Shrine === Access === * 15 minutes by car from {{ill|lt=Iwafune Station|Q11475586|WD=Q11475586|1=Iwafune Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Ryōmō Line|Q253391|WD=Q253391|lt=JR Ryōmō Line|1=Ryōmō Line}} * About 25 minutes by car from {{ill|lt=Sano-Fujioka Interchange|Q10885981|WD=Q10885981|lt=Sano-Fujioka I.C.|1=Sano-Fujioka interchange}} on the {{ill|lt=Tōhoku Expressway|Q428428|WD=Q428428|1=Tōhoku Expressway}} * 40 minutes from Tochigi Station on the JR Ryōmō Line / {{ill|lt=Tōbu Nikkō Line|Q845954|WD=Q845954|1=Tōbu Nikkō Line}} via the Tochigi City Fureai Bus Minagawa Hinokuchi Line bound for “Yurarakukan” ** Get off at “Murahi Shrine Entrance” bus stop, 5-minute walk (few services) ** Get off at “Eda Clinic-mae” bus stop, 20-minute walk === Notes === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book |Japanese |editor=Shigehide Ishihara |date=1964-02-11 |title=Tochigi Prefecture Shrine Gazetteer |publisher=Tochigi Prefectural Shrine Association |ref={{SfnRef|Ishihara|1964}} }} * {{Cite book |Japanese |editor=Iwafune Town Board of Education |date=1974-03-25 |title=History of Iwafune Town |publisher=Iwafune Town |ref={{SfnRef|Iwafune Town Board of Education|1974}} }} * {{Cite book |Japanese |editor=Hiroo Kazayama |date=1903-04-20 |title=History of Shrines in Shimotsuke: Volume 4 |publisher=Hiroo Kazayama |page=59 |ref={{SfnRef|Kazayama|1903}} }}{{National Diet Library Digital Collection|815435/1/61|format=NDLJP}} * {{Cite book |Japanese |editor=Morihiro Kōno |date=1893-10 |title=Shimotsuke Kokushi: Vol. 3 |publisher=Satō San’ichirō |page=12 |ref={{SfnRef|Kōno|1893}} }}{{National Diet Library Digital Collection|763928/1/13|format=NDLJP}} * {{Cite book |Japanese |author={{ill|lt=Ren’in Suzuka|Q6536446|WD=Q6536446|1=Suzuka Tsuratane}} (compiler) |coauthors={{ill|lt=Yorikuni Inoue|Q6535302|WD=Q6535302|1=Inoue Yorikuni}} and {{ill|lt=Ariyoshi Saeki|Q11382847|WD=Q11382847|1=Saeki Ariyoshi}} (editors) |date=1902-11-05 |title={{ill|lt=Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708|WD=Q11590708|1=Jinja Kakuroku}}: Lower Volume |publisher={{ill|lt=Kōten Kōkyūjo|Q11580997|WD=Q11580997|1=Koten Kokyusho}} |page=294 |ref={{SfnRef|Suzuka|1902}} }}{{National Diet
